speaker: interviewer
text: Shlomo Berger, in Los Angeles, California, USA. Shlomo, you started when you saw Hitler. As one of the people--

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, together with him, in his car, was sitting Mussolini, and actually the president of Hungary. His name was Horthy. They were in together, in the same group together with the Germans. Anyway, to make it short, I believe I was one of the very few Jews that saw Hitler in his real glory.

speaker: interviewer
text: You are, I think.

speaker: interviewee
text: Times were not getting any better, until finally, in December 1941, United States entered the war against Germany. And that year, they really started. They started to create ghettos. They started to-- to take Jews to the concentration camps. They started to kill Jews wherever they felt like it. They did not need any court orders to kill Jews or to arrest them. But we were not aware what was going on. Nobody could ever believe what was going to happen. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Until-- but we are still home. The parents are still home. The brothers are still home. They didn't have a ghetto in our town. We still went to work. And we used to work-- most of our work used to be-- we used to work for a German organization that used to be called Organisation Todt. They used to do the working-- they were there working brigades for the German army. But they were German. And we made a lot of connections with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: September-- August the 10th, 1942-- that is the day that goes around in infamy. Like-- like-- like Roosevelt said, at his speech when-- when the Japanese attacked the United States-- what does that mean? On Sunday, August the 9th, an order came out through the Judenrat that all Jews-- and that means all Jews-- have to report tomorrow morning, Monday morning, at 9 o'clock, to the marketplace for registration and resettlement.

speaker: interviewee
text: The same time, every Jewish house the German Gestapo put up a notice that, after 9 o'clock Monday morning, all Jews have to be out. The doors have to be open, take a-- a luggage of not more than 25 pounds, and come for the registration. And anybody that we caught in their houses will be shot. A panic started. Nobody knew what's going on. Some people went into hiding. Some people, I don't know what they did. We had no choice. We made a choice-- the family made a choice. We didn't know what's going to happen. But we are going to come to the registration. It came Monday morning, at 8 o'clock. Everybody took a little bundle. And my father, my mother, my sister Berta with her two children, her husband Jakobowitz, the four brothers, and some cousins of ours, we came out to the marketplace, and we're standing-- and ready for that registration. The door was left open. And we're standing and waiting. By approximately 9 o'clock, all of a sudden, trucks coming in, the Ukraine police, Polish police, German police, SS, they encircled the whole marketplace. We are standing in the middle, approximately 2,000 to 3,000 people, old, young, middle. We are standing and waiting and don't know what's going to happen. All of a sudden, they started the selection. They started to pick up all the old people, approximately 400 to 500 people. Put them in one side. The rest are still standing. Among these old people was my father. Trucks pulled up. They loaded them in the trucks. Whoever couldn't get on, they threw him on the trucks. And in the last truck, soldiers with-- with machine guns, and they drove away. We didn't know where, what, where, and when-- but it took approximately an hour and a half. They were back, empty. We found out later that there-- there was a mass grave prepared about 10 kilometers away, up in-- in the forest. The name of the place is Dukla. They all were shot over there, and put into a mass grave. And that's where I suspect my father is, still. The reason I know the place, because my brother Mike went back there. And he-- took a Pole, took him there, and showed him the place. And there was a monument standing there, in Hebrew and Polish, in which it says that in that and that day-- actually they-- they missed the date over there. They say the 13th of August. But 500 Jews were murdered here, and they are in the mass grave. Somebody keeps up the monument. Somebody keeps up the place. I don't know who. Somebody is paying for it, but it's being maintained. Now, coming to the next thing-- we are standing there and waiting. All of a sudden, small pickup trucks are coming up. It was a different type of German bosses, or whatever it is. And they came in a little pickup truck-- it was the chief of that Organisation Todt, that we were working. And he called off the names of the four brothers-- my brother-in-law with his one girl, the older girl, and a cousin of mine. Her name was Pessel Mandel. And they got the permission from the Gestapo to take us out from there. They called us up. They put us on that panel truck. They asked him whether my mother can go with us. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they-- so my mother says, boys, save yourself. Go. There's nothing we can do.

speaker: interviewee
text: They drove us away to the airport to work for the day. And during the time that-- that that registration, which they called the aktion, that was going on, the Germans established a ghetto. In five houses, one street, five or six houses and one street, during that time that we were there, they put up a gate at one side. They put up a gate on the other side. And that was supposed to be our place to live. Now we didn't know anything about what's happening over there. We worked all day at that airport. At night time, we didn't go back anymore to our house. And they took us to this ghetto. And there were approximately-- I don't know-- 500, 600 people. To-- to put them in-- in five, six houses, 10, 15 in a room. People were laying on the floor. People were laying on boxes, one on top of each other. But that was supposed to be our place to live. The next day, in the morning, they came. They took us to our shop, our former father's shop. But we didn't own the shop anymore. A Pole that used to work for us, he was given over the shop. And he became the boss. And we had to go to work over there, for the-- to work for the Organisation Todt, not getting paid. Brought us in the morning, took us back at nighttime to sleep in-- in-- in the-- in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now what happened with the rest of the people that they are standing there? Now they were standing there all day. August was a very hot day, no food, no water. According to what I-- what I heard-- because I was not present at it-- by nighttime, they took them to the railroad station, they loaded them into cattle-- cattle-- the cars.

speaker: interviewee
text: Train took off to the next station. They brought other transports, from other little towns all the way around. They loaded up the train. And the train went east. Now somebody hired a Pole to follow the train, to find out where they are going. And according to the information we received, the train went in to Belzec, which was not a concentration camp. It was just a [NON-ENGLISH] camp. That means the trains came in there--

speaker: interviewer
text: A death camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: A death camp. The trains came in there. They gassed them. There burned them. And there-- and there is no witness. I never heard about a single witness that can testify that he was in Belzec and came out alive. So my suspicion is that that's where the rest of my-- my family is. And that's why it could happen between the 10th of August, and the 15th of August. I don't know how long it took there. But I-- at that-- on the Jewish calendar, the 10th of August was Tisha B'Av, which is a-- a day of Jewish memorial of the-- of the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] of the destruction of the Jewish temple.

speaker: interviewer
text: The temple, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I celebrate our anniversary, our yahrzeit, what they say every year on Tisha B'Av for my whole family, for everybody that was there. To continue with my story, we were going on like that, every day to work, back and forth, until a couple months later. Sometimes in October, the four brothers were called to the Judenrat. And-- [INAUDIBLE] and they ordered us that the SS, which had-- which had a training camp in a place called Moderowka, which was 20 kilometers away, 18 kilometers away, they want to get 30 tailors to their camp, to make uniforms for the SS. And they want all the four Bergers to go. Now I did not intend to go there. So we started to argue. And he said, we got to finish our work for the Organisation Todt. We finally made a settlement. The two of us will go, and two of us will remain. So the four of us, the four brothers, had a conference. And we decided that my brother Joshua and I should remain. My brother Michael and Moses should go to the camp. They went there. And we stayed here. We still-- They were treated pretty good over there because this was not a concentration camp yet. And by the time-- during that time, that I was there, my brother and I, and my brother-in-law, made up our mind that we are going to run some place. We are going to-- to change our names, become Christians.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we had a friend over there. His name was Nagel. And he had connections with a Jewish printer. The Jewish printer was making stamps for the Gestapo. So he made himself an extra stamp, and he was selling false documents. So we got ourselves Polish documents. My name became Jan Jerzowski. Now what do the documents say? The document said, plain language, that I have been ordered to report to work for the Organisation Todt on the front line near Kiev. A [GERMAN], it was called in German. Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was all my document that I had, with a stamp of the Gestapo, and a stamp from the Organisation Todt. Sometime, in the first part of December, that Nagel, that I'm talking about, he was working in a German store, which was two doors-- two houses away from us. In the same building, upstairs, was the Gestapo. Every lunchtime, he used to come into our shop. And we used to talk-- we used to discuss.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now we have documents. He has documents. And we decided we are all going to run. We didn't know where. OK? So during that time that I already had Polish documents, there was a lady coming into our shop all the time, Polish lady. Her name-- her name was Mrs. Dukhowski. And she liked us.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she says to us, now listen boys-- if you ever get in trouble, you need to run. My husband is in charge of constructing a bridge on the Dniester River, in a place called Czortkow, and a place called Niemirow, that was near Stanislawow. If you have to run, go to him. Tell him who you-- what it's all about. And I'm quite sure he's going to help you. Well, she didn't give me no letter. She didn't give me-- but she said memorize the address, where it is.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, that Nagel came in. And he says Shlomo, something is happening. Too many Gestapo from all towns are coming again. Something is going to happen tomorrow.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we decided we're not going to sleep in the ghetto tonight. After it got dark, they took us back to the ghetto. We escape from the ghetto, nighttime, each one to a different place. I run up to the man's house that had our shop.

speaker: interviewee
text: I came in at nighttime. And I said, I've got to sleep over here tonight. He didn't want to keep me there, but I force myself in. I says, I'll be in the attic. I slept overnight there. Stayed overnight actually, didn't sleep. In the morning, he went down to town to see what's happening. He comes back and he says, Shlomo, the ghetto's surrounded. They're shooting the Jews-- trucks are there, they're taking them out. You must leave. And I says, I'm not going to leave now in day time. And he wanted to chase me out. So me, having no choice, I blackmailed him. I says, you throw me out now and I'm going to tell them you hid me here overnight. But I promise you, the minute it gets dark, I'm going to leave. Well, he had no choice. I had no choice. And it got dark. I went through the fields-- snow, it was already winter. I went through the fields to the next little town. I didn't want-- I didn't dare to go to the railroad station in our town. The name of the place was Iwonicz. I bought a ticket-- I bought a ticket to Stanislawow. I boarded the train, and I left. During the time of the train, many times, they came and asked for identification. And whenever I showed this document, everything was OK. The train went as far as a town that's called Rejowiec. And then you had to get off the train, and wait for another six hours to another train. My shoes-- my-- my socks were wet from the snow. I had no luggage.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I started walking on the street, waiting for the next train. So I'm trying to find out where there are some Jews living. So I see a boy walking around-- there was an armband on his hand. So I said, where are the Jews? He says, follow me.

speaker: interviewee
text: I followed him to the ghetto-- it was a ghetto already in that city, Rejowiec-- but it wasn't locked. People used to come in and out. And I came into that place, I sat down, I took off my shoes, dried it out. Gave me something to eat. I spent there, approximately, three, four hours. And finally, I'm going back to the train. I come out. That boy that led me in there is still waiting for me outside.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he says, I couldn't leave without telling you what I'm going to tell you right now. I said, what you want to tell me? He says, I just want to tell you that if I didn't recognize you that you were a Jew, nobody else will. You're going to survive the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's-- this is-- I boarded the train. I kept going. I came to that place, Czortkow. I looked up that man, Dukhowski, his name-- the name is-- his name was Tadeusz Dukhowski. I looked him up, I came in to him, waited for him to come home from work.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I say to him, do you know me? He says, no. And I say, you-- you know, I am Jacob Berger's son. Oh, I know your father, he says. So what can I do for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I told him what his wife told me. I showed him my documents, what I have. I says, you need to help me. And he went ahead, and he says, well I'm going to do the best I can. He says, you cannot stay in Czortkow. He says, there is a SS training camp in here. They might recognize you. But I have another place, it's called Niemirow that's about 15, 20 kilometers from Stanislawow, east of Stanislawow. And he says, I'm gonna put you up there. I can't pay you, but we're going to pretend that you are a Pole that came from-- that was sent by the company. And you're working here.

speaker: interviewer
text: So every day, I went to the bridge. I was carrying some papers, making some notes, pretending that I'm a big macha there. And-- and he took me to the Ukraine police. And he registered me as a Pole, Jan Jerzowski.

speaker: interviewee
text: Came to-- to-- gave me the ration cards. And I acted-- started to act like a Pole. Every day, I went to church. I was-- they had these hand organs. I used to pump the hand organs. I got acquainted with the priest. The priest wanted me to come for the communion, and come for-- what do they call this--

speaker: interviewer
text: Sermons.

speaker: interviewee
text: That you tell the priest-- anyway--

speaker: interviewer
text: Confession.

speaker: interviewee
text: Confession. I didn't dare to do that. So I told him, I was just for confession the next town. Yeah, I already did it. And I also got acquainted with a Polish woman there, a former teacher, a young woman. that I told her a story that I am an escapee from the Gestapo. She did not know that I'm Jewish. And I started to organize myself as a-- as a Pole, to being known in the community-- a small little community. At one time, an order came out from the Gestapo. There were no Jews anymore-- in the [INAUDIBLE]. An order came out from the Gestapo that all Poles have to have an internal passport, which was called the kennkarte, issued by the Gestapo. Unable to get that, you have to have a birth certificate.

speaker: interviewer
text: How am I going to get a birth certificate? Anyway, that Christina and I convinced the priest that he gave me a birth certificate, that my name is Jan Jerzowski. I was born over there, the place. And he put down these tiny little letters on the bottom that that was issued on a verbal testimony. He covered himself.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had enough nerve. I took that birth certificate, I walked into Stanislawow, to the Gestapo, and got an official kennkarte-- a legal-- a legal passport. Now I'm already a legal person, in case I get stopped. I have a legal passport. That lasted until approximately March, 1943. No, March 1943-- OK. The bridge was finally finished. Now, their company was going back to-- to western Poland. There's no way I could go with them. And there was no way that Dukhowski would take me with him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we had a conversation. We got together. And he says-- they used to call me Jasiu-- Jan, short in-- in Polish is Jasiu. He said, Jasiu, I did what I could for you. The company is finished here. We're leaving. You can't go with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, from now on, you are on your own. The only favor I have to ask you, if you ever get caught, remember I never knew who you were. Don't put me in.

speaker: interviewer
text: They left. And Arbeitsamt, which is the-- the-- the office of-- Of the workers.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of the workers-- providing workers, came an order that all Poles that got out of the jobs in here, had to come to register to go to work in Germany. And these local Poles did not want to go to Germany. So they decided that they're going to run away into the forest. That was all forest lands. And since I'm a Pole, and I don't want to go to Germany either, so I run away to the forest together with them. And whether they liked it or not, we became part of the group, a resistance group--

speaker: interviewer
text: Partisans.

speaker: interviewee
text: --a Polish resistance group of approximately 100 Poles.

speaker: interviewer
text: Catholic Poles.

speaker: interviewee
text: Catholic poles. Now the Poles had hidden arms from before. These were their homes. So they dug out their arms. Everyone got a gun or a rifle, not too much ammunition. And we stayed in the forests, never contacted in open-- open warfare. But we became a nuisance. We became a nuisance to the German military. Now, whenever we could help, we used to mine the railroad tracks. We picked a place where there was a curve that they couldn't see from far away. When we saw a transport going, we put up some-- some mines under the-- under the railroad tracks. And they used to explode the trains. We used to grab whatever we can. And we ran. At nighttime, sometimes we came into the villages. And we slept at the villages. The minute a German patrol was coming, we knew in advance. Never engaged-- we never engaged in open warfare. Actually--

speaker: interviewer
text: Sabotage.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sabotage. Actually, what the-- the whole group had one thing in mind-- the war is going to be over sometimes. And nobody knew what's going to happen. But it will be over.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were the only Jew [? between there ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, but as a Pole.

speaker: interviewer
text: As a Pole, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: As a Pole.

speaker: interviewer
text: But the only--

speaker: interviewee
text: The only Jew. In other words, the only thing is the Poles were such good people over there. I mean, good in-- in-- in a bad sense of words. They used to say my-- my-- that was-- that were my friends, the fighters.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: They used to say, there's one thing Hitler did a good job, that he killed the Jews. He got rid of Poland of the Jews. As much as they didn't like the Germans, they worse liked the Jews. I was very careful not to find out-- that-- for them not to find out who I am. We used to drink a lot of these homemade vodka. I never knew how to drink, but in able to pretend that I can drink better than them, I used to drink tall glasses of vodka. Just sneak out to the side, put my fingers in my-- in my throat, and throw it out, so that I can go in next. My mind had to work day and night. And one day in the summer, 1943, we heard that something is happening in Stanislawow. We didn't know what. The group wanted to find out what was going to happen.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they knew that I can speak some German. I didn't speak too much. But they knew that I can speak some German. I was not in the leadership. Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they ordered me to take the train. Since they knew I have a passport--

speaker: interviewer
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewee
text: --they ordered me to go to the train and go to Stanislawow. They made up a false document, false little paper, that I'm requested to appear for the Arbeitsamt in Stanislawow-- which was the main city over there. I took the train. I come to Stanislawow. While the train stopped at the station, we looked at a [NON-ENGLISH], I mean, a line of police on both sides of the train. There was somebody coming up with a bullhorn. Nobody coming out from the train-- everybody comes out through one door.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I did not wait to be the last. I got out from the train, among the first one going through that-- that-- that-- that line of police. Coming into the front door, and here is a Gestapo man standing, and a Ukraine police, and some other civilian German police. And they ask me-- and I'm holding my-- my-- my passport in my hand. And I'm holding that the order to come for the Arbeitsamt. And the German asked, [GERMAN]? I don't understand German. Polish. Keep that thought. We have to change tape-- the tape. Now we are going to--
